term: FIEA (Financial Instruments and Exchange Act / 金融商品取引法)
plain_definition: Japan's principal securities law -- broadly the equivalent of Hong Kong's Securities and Futures Ordinance -- covering disclosure, broker-dealer and asset-management licensing, market conduct, and, since a 2019 amendment, tokenized securities. Unlike crypto-asset exchange business or digital-money-type stablecoins, which sit in the Payment Services Act, Japan regulates security tokens by treating them as ordinary FIEA securities carrying a digital 'electronically recorded transferable right' wrapper, layering full prospectus-style disclosure and Type I broker-dealer registration onto instruments that would otherwise get lighter treatment.
formal_definition: 金融商品取引法, Act No. 25 of 1948 (昭和二十三年法律第二十五号), promulgated 13 April 1948 -- confirmed by direct fetch of the Act's current consolidated text via the e-Gov Law API this session (law_id 323AC0000000025). Art. 2(2) enumerates instruments the Act deems securities ('みなし有価証券'), including trust beneficial interests and collective investment scheme interests. Art. 2(3), inserted by Act No. 28 of 2019, effective 1 May 2020, reclassifies such an instrument as an 'electronically recorded transferable right' (電子記録移転権利, ERTR) -- and therefore a first-paragraph, Art. 2(1)-equivalent security -- once it is tokenized on an electronically transferable system. Art. 4(1) requires a securities registration statement for a public offering of Art. 2(1) securities including ERTRs; Art. 29 requires Type I Financial Instruments Business Operator registration to deal in or broker them. A pending 2026 bill (submitted to the Diet 10 April 2026; passed the House of Representatives 11 June 2026; pending in the House of Councillors as of this research session) would migrate crypto-asset business regulation from the PSA into FIEA -- not yet enacted, and not asserted here as current law.
